transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hey, Bailey, how's it going?
Good.
Happy New Year.
Happy New Year to you, too.
How are you all doing with the post-holiday?
It's rough.
It's a rough week.
It's a rough week, I think, for everybody, but we're getting there.
We're getting there.
My brain is slowly coming back online.
I know.
Luckily, we're all in the same boat, kind of trying to remember who we are and what we do.
Exactly.
Exactly.
Yeah.
I mean, for me, there's such a backlog now of articles to review, which has been a blessing and a curse for me to try to go through them.
And I've been trying to pay special attention since it was all those comparison ones.
But I think they're all looking good now that we've kind of gone through that second layer of scrutiny.
So I'm feeling pretty good about that.
Yeah, that's great to know.
And I think it's a good, we have that sort of pipeline ready to go now.
So I think in the future, if we want to prioritize more of those comparison articles, it probably shouldn't need that kind of second round of scrutiny.
Okay, that's perfect.
Yeah, I think the volume that we have now is a good place to start.
I don't think I'll add any more quite yet because I think we've kind of got our most important bases covered with the ones that we've done.
But yeah, kind of be curious to see how those ones do and then maybe circle back and do some more as well.
Yeah, for sure.
Cool.
Hi, Bethany.
Happy New Year.
Hi, Happy New Year.
I'm just to share our agenda.
I'm just
Should joining today?
Should we wait for her?
I don't think she's joining today.
No, we can go ahead.
Okay.
All right.
So this week, welcome back, back to the world post-holiday.
I think we should have just shared today this week's articles for your review.
I also wanted to check in about kind of article topics.
So we'll need some more of those approved.
I think we only have one right now.
Kind of in our content calendar.
I did.
I did 10 more yesterday.
I approved 10 more.
So we should be all set there.
Perfect.

And I also just wanted to kind of generally check in because I know, I think that Katie was planning to kind of check in with on the accounting and bookkeeping topics, if I remember, to kind of get a general overview of how those were.
So I just wanted to check in on sort of the balance of the clusters, see if that's kind of still where we wanted the main focus for now.
Okay.
Thank
And just kind of do a general check-in.
I don't think, moving forward as we kind of generate more topics, I don't think accountants and bookkeepers stuff should be the focus.
I think that should maybe be, you know, an equal, that that can have an equal balance with the other topic clusters that we have.
I'll take another look and kind of do a deep dive on what's still there and get back to you.
But I think we have a good runway now of new articles.
And I think if we just kind of, that will probably continue to be, you know, a quarter or a fifth of the stuff we approve moving forward.
There were a bunch more of those that I did approve yesterday.
And I think that'll put us in a nice position.
But I'll review and let you know if I have any concerns.
Okay, perfect.
Yeah, and I'll take a look at what you've kind of approved and go in and just make sure the balance is still good.
Because I know we had done a bunch of new keyword research around the accounting and bookkeeping topic.
So just to make sure we have kind of enough spread over all of those topics.
Thanks, Sam.
Sam.
We can take a look next week as well.
Awesome.
So then I know we shared sort of this kind of content expansion proposal.
We dropped it on you right before the holidays.
So I didn't know if your team had had a chance to take a look, if you had any questions that you wanted me to answer or kind of take to our team or anything else you wanted kind of more detail on.
But I realize you're just getting back in the office.
So you may not have had a chance to review yet.
I have not.
But I will dig into it in the next week or so.
And we'll definitely let you know if I've got questions.
OK, perfect.
And I will say that it's pretty, when you take a look, it gets pretty broad in terms of how we're sort of defining these options.
So I will say that I think it's just to kind of give you a sense of what that expansion can look like.
But it obviously depends a lot on what kind of content you're looking.
OK.
just
So we're open, you know, we wanted to share this as a starting point.
But as you kind of define your 2026 goals and what your plan is, you know, we're happy to kind of adjust and think through what kind of content you all are looking to expand into.
Makes sense.
Okay.

And then the other sort of planning piece is we are kind of trying to schedule 2026 planning sessions across clients just to get a sense of, you know, your KPIs, you know, your focus for content and Q1 and all the ways we can support.
I think obviously this will kind of directly tie to this.

But as you have kind of defined those kind of KPIs and have those conversations internally, like we'd love to sort of set some time to get a deeper dive into what you're focused on this year and this quarter and kind of understand how we can support.
So I think a lot of clients were sort of.
Scheduling these out, I think, towards the end of the month as these conversations are happening internally now.
But if there's kind of timing that makes sense for you to put this on the calendar, let me know.
And I would love to schedule that and make sure we have the right people from our team there to help think through strategy.
Sure.
Yeah, I think this, Dave, you'll take the lead on this, but maybe you and I can meet on maybe Monday and just go through our notes.
I think I have a pretty strong understanding of what we want to do, but I want to make sure Dave and I kind of meet, too.
It's funny, we've done a lot of our annual planning.
It's mostly done, but now we're working on kind of bringing the full team into it.
So I do think let us kind of figure out next week what might work, and then we'll grab some time.
Perfect.
That sounds great.
So, yeah, I'll follow up then.
Well, it sounds like you're very on top of things.
Trying, trying.
And then I know we also, I was out, but I know that Jacob also sort of shared the monthly deep dive for most of the month for December in that reporting document.
And I took a look at it and, you know, it looks like good progress.
But if you had any other kind of questions around that, let me know.
And we're happy to kind of dive into that since I know it was a bit of an incomplete month with the holidays and that coming a little bit early.
I'm not sure if I saw that one yet with the holiday chaos, but I'm going to take another look.
Yeah, okay.
So take a look and let us know if you have any questions around that.
But the sort of overview was that it looks good and looks quite healthy, even with the kind of usual kind of weird dip or weird behavior of traffic, I think, in December around the holiday time.
Oh, and then in terms of ops on our end, we are.
We're sort of shifting the way we kind of do our meetings and updates a little bit on our end that I wanted to touch base with you on.
We're shifting to, we're planning to shift to sort of a bi-weekly meeting cadence.
So we can use this time, I think, to focus more on reporting and strategic insights and kind of content performance.

And obviously, we'll continue to talk about any editorial feedback, but I think as we sort of move to a steady state of delivery, we're hoping to use kind of Slack for those more async, just project management updates, and then focus our meeting time.
And if we want to meet less frequently, but even for longer, to kind of focus that more on how we can kind of be your best strategic partner.
I think that's sort of for a steady state.
I think if we are talking about, you know, content expansion or new projects and that.
I think we would likely, you know, maintain the weekly cadence until we sort of reached a steady state in whatever project we're on.

So obviously there's some flexibility there, but just to kind of give you a heads up, you know, I think over this January, we're kind of planning to move to that kind of biweekly cadence for regular check-ins.
Cool.
No, that works.
I think we're in a good steady state now.
That makes sense.
So that sounds good.
So we can kind of sort of see how that goes depending on whether we expand and then do like more ad hoc meetings as well.
I think that was it on my end.
Was there anything else you all wanted to touch base on?
Yeah, a couple quick things.
Yeah.
Just sort of on that same note, I think one thing that I want to spend a little more time thinking about is just kind of rethinking.
We're taking a fresh look at the type of content that we're producing and kind of expanding our horizons a little bit.
like we talked about the risk of it getting a little samey, and I want to make sure that we're kind of venturing into new territory with the stuff we're doing.
So that's something that's on my mind that I want to make sure that we're diving into in the new year.
So yeah, more to come there.

And I think I'd love to see any recommendations you guys have based on what we've done and what's working, where we might kind of shift our focus a little bit and expand the horizons a little bit into new areas.

The other thing I wanted to flag is that, and Bethany, you're not even aware of this yet because this just came down, but we had some positive traction in how we can place our disclosures on the pages.
So Thread has given us the okay, at least for now, to rather than having the inline disclosures on pages, we can do a superscript that links down to the bottom of the article.
Ooh, that's exciting.
dedicated disclosure area.
Yeah, so it should make things a lot cleaner on page.
year in thread.
Yeah, we'll see how, you know, they shift their guidelines so frequently.

But it will, it just kind of introduces a new, I wouldn't call it a complication, but just in how we're, how I'm kind of giving the written articles to you and then how those are getting put into Contentful.
The way I have it now is that we kind of have to have at the very bottom an H3 heading that's disclosures and then we anchor text from those superscripts down to that part of the page.
So I'm just kind of now putting those into the Google Docs with those hyperlinks, jump links down to that part of the doc.
So it's just something to flag that that's, that will kind of need to be translated into when that moves over to Contentful.
Yeah, that's exciting.
I feel like that will look much clearer.
Cleaner.
Do you have, I'm wondering, I think we should be able to update that on our end, and we just sort of need a set of rules that we can use to update the system.

So I'm wondering if you have captured, just kind of in writing anywhere, what their new requirements are, and we can use that to update our pipeline so that, you know, hopefully after these updates, you shouldn't have to do that manually.
Definitely.
And it's a bit of a, I think it's a bit of a moving target as we, as we kind of submit things to them, you know, with different instances and use cases for how we're doing it.
They're kind of maybe push back and make some changes.
So it'll be a bit of a shifting thing, but I can, I can write up something as I understand it, but they're, what they're looking for now and send that over.

Another, similarly, I think because there's always these ongoing changes, it's another thing that I really want to make sure we're staying on top of is how we're, like the rules that we're incorporating and how we're updating those rules.
Like another thing that happened recently was we made a small change to our...
And I just want to make sure that we have a kind of a structure in place that to make sure that we're able to stay on top of that.
So is the best thing just to kind of have a running doc that I can update as needed and flag for you when kind of changes like this happen?
Or what would be the most sort of straightforward way to stay on top of things like that?

Yeah, I mean, I think we can go either way, even when even when you flag things in line in feedback, you know, making it clear that it's sort of a universal change means that we will then go through and kind of update our writing guidelines, our company context anywhere where that appears.

But I think if, you know, especially if there's kind of a list of changes that, know, be updated on kind of a semi-regular basis, then it might make sense to have sort of one master document where those appear.
So there's kind of one source of truth that you know how to to go and update instead of.
Yeah, that'd be great.
I also, this just brought up another question in my mind.
Do we have access to kind of the documents that you're using to inform all of these things?

Just because like with all of the kind of ad hoc changes that happen and little things over time, I just want to make sure that, you know, we're not building this sort of like, you know, tech debt type of thing where we're adding more than we need or things are shifting in a weird way.
And I want to make sure we can kind of keep it grounded with, you know, very clear guidelines.
Is that something that we can see just to make sure we're kind of staying on top of what we want?
All of those should be available in Notion.
I will go through and make sure they've been updated because I think with the holidays, basically, Atlas are kind of the pipeline that actually creates the content.
doesn't pull directly from Notion.
So when we make those updates in our system, we then kind of manually update the documents in Notion just to make sure you have access to them.
So I will go through today and.
Make sure those are updated because I feel like with the holidays, sometimes those incremental changes don't get captured.
So let me make sure those are up to date.
And then you can kind of take a look and review what we have now.
And then we can create a new document that if that makes sense to have that sort of be the repository for those kind of numbers or any data that gets updated on a semi-regular basis.
And that way you have a place to go to kind of update those changes.
Perfect.
Yeah.
Yeah, that sounds great.

Just over time, it's sort of occurred to me that I want to make sure that we're keeping this really tight and strong and not kind of just, you know, gradually degrading to a place where it's not really, you know, functioning at its best.
So that sounds good.
And then I guess I would love to dig a little bit more into kind of your question around fresh content or fresh content formats to understand kind of a little bit more, I think, how.

Yeah.
How do I?
I think, you know, when I look at sort of the, you know, the assignments, you know, the assignment pitches that are coming through, a lot of them are great.
A lot of the time they start to feel a little too similar.
just want to kind of, you know, move the, you know, open up the bucket a little bit to experiment.

would experiment, I think is what we'd want to do and just kind of like test new, test new categories and not just, not just keep.

So penned into the same, you know, types of topics and just see if there's other things that are maybe a little more tangentially related to small business owners that will tap into that, into that audience a little, a little, you know, maybe a little bit indirectly.
And just, yeah, just kind of experiment and play with different, different topics that, that don't necessarily fit so cleanly into those buckets that we've already established.
Does that make sense to you also, Bethany?
I it's almost more of like a strategy shift in terms of like experimenting with new keywords and like maybe a shift in strategy versus like format, I guess was my question.
I actually think it's probably both.
Like I think we are, especially if we expand content, going to want to always be playing with like new areas where we might want to produce content, like new content topics.
For formats, I think the question in my mind is like, how, how far can we get?
Just kind of the same basic article, right?
Like having a really dynamic and engaging blog requires, you know, multiple formats.
So, you know, it could be interviews.
It could be just structuring the article in a different way.
could be trying listicles.
It could be quizzes.
It could be that it's, you know, some other format that we haven't even thought about.
And I think, you know, maybe the first question is, what's the comfort level with that from your end?
Like if your bread and butter is this kind of core article format, great.
And, you know, we know then that if we are going to diversify, we'll have to do it internally.
So maybe that's the first question.
What formats can you support?
And maybe the other question with that question is, you know, should we brainstorm together?
Or are you already doing anything interesting with other clients from a format perspective?
Um, cause you know, if, if, like I said, if you're kind of like, well, this is what we do, it's what we're great at, that's fine.
And then like, we will know to kind of go out and find some kind of supplemental ways to diversify.
Yeah, that's really helpful context.

Um, and I think this will be helpful for me too, cause I can sort of take this back, um, and dig in a little bit more to see what we're doing kind of across accounts and see if there's anything that might fit the bill for this.
Yeah.

I mean, I would love to know, like, if there's anything that you know that, you know, sort of the pipeline is just really good at producing and like does the really great job at like that, that we're not really even considering.
I think that would be a great to experiment with and dive into.
Yeah.
Yeah, for sure.

I mean, I think I think that a lot of the places that we've seen success are places where, um, like velocity and just like building a library of content is a place that we're really good at plugging in and playing.
Like, you know, um, kind of creating databases of content where what you want is.
Kind of very, like a large database of content that can speak to very specific questions and search intent.

think things like very high, kind of high-touch content, like interactive quizzes, things that take a lot of kind of development and engineering support are not usually places that kind of we play because it's just not where our pipeline can kind of have the most impact, I think.

So I think one place may be kind of bringing in director-level support who are kind of expert-level kind of at identifying these opportunities, places to create these more databases and libraries of content.
I think that may be one place to kind of bring in someone to take a look at what you have now and kind of help identify those opportunities.
And then I think the second thing would be going back and sort of seeing what we're doing across.

All these client accounts as we kind of experiment with different formats and see if there are other projects that we're doing and places we've built Pipeline and kind of had success that we can hopefully then take that and kind of adapt it for your context.

Yeah, mean, as a takeaway, I would love it if it would be possible to get just some links to other things that you built just as a bit of a thought starter for us to kind of see what's possible and where we could potentially, you know, what kind of direction we could head in.

Yeah, I think that makes sense and sort of some of these more programmatic or templated libraries of content could be a good place to start because I know you had mentioned you had a help center and some other places that might be a good fit for that.

So why don't I, I'll pull together some examples and then kind of also take that back to our director team and see if they have some suggestions of some good places that we might be able to expand into kind of different formats beyond this kind of more standard editorial.

then I think that'll be great also to kind of bring to this planning session as we think about your goals in 2026 as well.
